race.name: Vuelta Puerto Rico
race.tagline: A supported tour, not a race — circumnavigate Puerto Rico in three days.

vitals.location: San Juan (start/finish), full island circumnavigation, Puerto Rico
vitals.entry_fee: 2026: $795 (early) rising to $1,195, plus taxes and ACTIVE.com registration fees
vitals.feed_zones: Rest stops spaced 20-25 miles apart with water, sports drink, and snacks

climate.description: February in Puerto Rico: average highs around 83°F, brief but possible rain showers, consistent humidity and sun exposure across all three days.
challenges: Heat and humidity: sustained tropical exposure over 375 miles/three days
Sun: high UV exposure, riders advised on SPF and hydration

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 53. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 51. FLAG (wave 10, not applied): sol review notes several dimensions sit awkwardly against wave-10 research — distance=4 vs. rubric-5 for a 604km aggregate; climbing=2 vs. the ~12,000ft/3,658m aggregate cited in the tour's own (older) materials; road_surface=1 (pristine tarmac) vs. cited older pothole reports; organization=3 assumes timed-race closures for what is an explicitly untimed supported tour; field_depth=2 implies rankings the event doesn't have; expenses=2 looks low against a $795-$1,195 entry before flights/lodging. Numbers left unchanged pending human review.

course_description.character: A fully supported, non-racing three-day tour that circumnavigates the entire island clockwise from San Juan: east through Luquillo and Fajardo, south along the coast to Ponce, west across the dry southwest through Guánica, La Parguera and Cabo Rojo to Mayagüez/Rincón, then back around the west and north coasts through Aguadilla, Arecibo, Manatí and Dorado to Old San Juan. Riders self-sort into three controlled pelotons by pace — roughly 21 mph, 18 mph, and 16 mph averages — with mechanical, medical, and luggage support throughout. There is no competitive classification; it's an endurance tour framed as a 'pro-like' peloton experience, not a timed race.
course_description.signature_challenge: Simply finishing 375 miles across three consecutive days in tropical heat and humidity, holding a controlled peloton pace for hours at a stretch.
suffering_zones: The dry southwest coast (Guánica to Cabo Rojo): exposed, hot, and long between towns.
Cumulative fatigue over three back-to-back full days — the tour's own materials put total climbing around 12,000 ft across the three days, mostly gradual but with a handful of steep short pitches.

logistics.lodging_strategy: Not included in registration; participants arrange their own lodging in San Juan and each night's stopover town along the route.
logistics.transport: Fully supported: rest-stop vehicles, mechanical support (light adjustments included, parts/major repairs cash-only), medical/EMS on course, and transport back to your hotel if you can't complete a day.
logistics.food: Breakfast and lunch stops provided daily; rest stops every 20-25 miles with water, sports drink, fruit, and snacks.

history.origin_story: Founded in 2006 by William Medina as 'La Vuelta de los Nueve Faros de Puerto Rico' (the Nine Lighthouses tour), with 89 riders in its inaugural three-day edition. The event is organized by Puerto Rico 360, Inc. and has run as an annual Presidents Day weekend fixture since.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Real, Long-Running Supported Tour — Not a Race, and It Doesn't Pretend to Be
biased_opinion.summary: Vuelta Puerto Rico is a legitimate, two-decade-old fixture: a fully supported, three-day, 375-mile clockwise circumnavigation of the island, run every February by the same organizer since 2006. It's honest about its format — three pace-based pelotons (roughly 16/18/21 mph averages), no competitive classification, no podium. The logistics are real (rest stops every 20-25 miles, mechanical/medical support, luggage transfer), and the route is a genuine island-spanning tour through both tourist coastline and the drier, less-visited southwest. Older rider reports flag real friction points worth knowing before you sign up: road-surface hazards, uneven advance disclosure of steeper pitches, and unusually strict no-refund/no-transfer terms.
strengths: Long track record — organized annually since 2006 by the same operator, with a real inaugural-edition history
Genuinely supported: rest stops every 20-25 miles, mechanical and medical support on course, luggage transfer, and three pace groups so riders of different abilities can hold a sustainable pace
A real full-island route, not just the tourist coastline — it crosses the drier, less-touristed southwest between Ponce and Mayagüez
Honest about format: marketed and run as a supported endurance tour, not dressed up as a competitive race
weaknesses: Not a race — no competitive classification or ranked results, despite the 'pro-like peloton' marketing framing
Entry terms are unusually strict: non-transferable, non-refundable, and non-deferrable without buying optional ACTIVE Refund coverage

Older rider reports describe real friction: pothole-related crashes, late luggage, too few portable toilets, and a roughly 17% climb some riders felt was under-disclosed in advance — worth asking the organizer about current-year conditions rather than assuming they're fixed
Total cost is steep once flights, lodging for three overnight towns, and the $795-$1,195 entry are added up
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A credible, long-running option if you want a genuine multi-day island tour with real support infrastructure and don't need a competitive result. Go in with clear expectations on the strict refund policy and ask current-year questions about road conditions and pace-group discipline before committing.

final_verdict.one_liner: A fully supported, non-competitive three-day circumnavigation of Puerto Rico — real logistics, real history, no podium.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Sign up if you want a genuine multi-day, fully supported island tour and are comfortable with a non-refundable entry. Skip it if you want a timed, competitive event, or if the strict cancellation terms are a dealbreaker.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a competitive island tour with real classification, look at a UCI Gran Fondo World Series stop instead. For a similarly supported multi-day tour format in the U.S., Seattle-to-Portland or Ride Across Wisconsin offer comparable fully-supported, non-competitive structures.

videos.title: La Vuelta Puerto Rico - 3 day cycling event
videos.description:
La Vuelta Puerto Rico is the ultimate cycling adventure for serious endurance athletes interested in a unique pro-like experience. Circumnavigate the entire island as part of a fast-paced international peloton on this fully supported, three-day, 375-mile event. Choose your group and ride at 15, 18 or 21 mph average.

Register here: http://www.vueltapr.com/registration
Connect on Facebook: http://www.facebook.com/lavueltapuertorico
Follow on Twitter: http://twitter.com/VueltaPR

The Peloton
If you've ever watched the Tour de France and thought about how great it would be to be part of a peloton of skilled riders as you glide effortlessly through pristine countryside, you're in luck. La Vuelta Puerto Rico is all about the peloton (and, yes, peloton riding is every bit as fun as it looks).

At La Vuelta, riders divide themselves into three pelotons based on the pace they prefer to ride. Group A is for the hammerheads, and travels at sustained speeds of more than 25 to 30 mph (a pace very similar to professional stage racers). Group B rides between 20 to 25 mph averaging 18 mph. Group C rides at 18 to 20 mph and averages 15 mph. Riders are expected to be able to maintain these speeds (Groups B & C) for the entirety of the event in order to keep up with the averages of 18 mph and 15 mph respectively.

Topography
Total elevation gain for all three days is approximately 12,000 feet. Expect all kinds of terrain, from flats to rolling hills to climbs. The majority of the climbs are slow and steady, but there are also a few short pitches that are quite steep (you'll know them when you see them).

The Weather
In winter, the average high temperature is 83°F.  Rain showers are always possible, but are usually brief.  Be prepared for sunshine and humidity most of the ride. Staying hydrated is essential and for that we provide sports drinks from our sponsors. It is also important to use high-SPF sun block and wear appropriate clothing. Headbands, cycling caps and white "arm warmers" are extremely popular accessories among seasoned Vuelta veterans.

Support
La Vuelta is a fully supported bike tour. Rest stops are spaced between 20 and 25 miles apart and stocked with water, sport drinks, fruit and other snacks. There are designated breakfast and lunch stops where riders will find delicious meals planned specifically for endurance athletes.

Support vehicles will be available for bicycle emergencies. Mechanical support will be offered on the course by professional bicycle mechanics. Services included in your registration are for "light" mechanical adjustments.  Additional parts and services are available on a cash-only basis.

Medical and EMS support will be available throughout the entire Vuelta. Cyclists will be provided with emergency contact numbers for medical or mechanical assistance before the tour begins.

In the unlikely event that you are unable to complete a particular day of the tour, you will be offered transportation to your hotel. Should you need to completely withdraw from the tour, our staff will assist you with arranging transportation for your return to San Juan.

Equipment/Requirements
Rider numbers -- we provide each rider with a number that will help us recognize you in the event of an emergency.  It will also identify you as an official rider in the Vuelta to other riders, police, etc.  The number sheet should be pinned to the back of your cycling jersey each day. Same goes with a helmet number and a bike number. 

Tires -- Due to the varied nature of the roadways on the island, we recommend using puncture-resistant all-season tires. 25 mm are very popular among seasoned Vuelta riders. Many others use kevlar lining for extra protection and all tires should be at the maximum pressure recommended by the manufacturer. 

Lights -- Due to the early-morning departures, cyclists must have a white light affixed to the front of their bike, as well as a red light affixed to the back. No exceptions.

Helmets -- Riders are required to wear appropriate safety helmets at all times, its the Law in Puerto Rico.

Cell phones -- Riders should carry cell phones in case of emergency. Speaking over the phone while riding is prohibited unless you use a bluetooth wireless device. 

Radios and headphones -- The use of radios and headphones while riding is prohibited. This includes ipods.  

Aero bars -- The use of aero bars is prohibited. Please remove them from your bikes prior to the event. We have a zero tolerance against these for obvious reasons.

Promotional Film produced by CYCLEFILM - http://cyclefilm.com
Cyclefilm is a dedicated cycling production company for cycling related DVDs, how-to cycling guides, cycling documentaries, L'Etape du Tour, Gran Fondo recon films, promotional videos and bicycle industry news coverage.
Connect on Facebook: http://www.facebook.com/cyclefilmfan
Follow on Twitter: http://twitter.com/cyclefilm

eligibility.notes: 2026 edition confirmed February 15-17, official site live with current pricing ($795-$1,195) and ACTIVE.com registration. No 2027 date posted yet as of verification. Verified 2026-07-24 via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research, editorial wave 10.
